infobox.value: | * [Affinity](/wiki/Affinity_(sociology) "Affinity (sociology)") * [Collectivism and individualism](/wiki/Collectivism_and_individualism "Collectivism and individualism") * [Community engagement](/wiki/Community_engagement "Community engagement") * [Community education](/wiki/Community_education "Community education") * [Community cohesion](/wiki/Community_cohesion "Community cohesion") * [Community of interest](/wiki/Community_of_interest "Community of interest") * [Community practice](/wiki/Community_practice "Community practice") * [Community of practice](/wiki/Community_of_practice "Community of practice") * [Community of place](/wiki/Community_of_place "Community of place") * [Community service](/wiki/Community_service "Community service") * [Communitarianism](/wiki/Communitarianism "Communitarianism") * [Community politics](/wiki/Community_politics "Community politics") * [Group cohesiveness](/wiki/Group_cohesiveness "Group cohesiveness") * [Group dynamics](/wiki/Group_dynamics "Group dynamics") * [Imagined community](/wiki/Imagined_community "Imagined community") * [Organization](/wiki/Organization "Organization") * [Prosocial behavior](/wiki/Prosocial_behavior "Prosocial behavior") * [Sense of community](/wiki/Sense_of_community "Sense of community") * [Small-group communication](/wiki/Small-group_communication "Small-group communication") * [Sobornost](/wiki/Sobornost "Sobornost") * [Social alienation](/wiki/Social_alienation "Social alienation") * [Social capital](/wiki/Social_capital "Social capital") * [Social death](/wiki/Social_death "Social death") * [Social exclusion](/wiki/Social_exclusion "Social exclusion") * [Socialization](/wiki/Socialization "Socialization") * [Social group](/wiki/Social_group "Social group") * [Social rejection](/wiki/Social_rejection "Social rejection") * [Social support](/wiki/Social_support "Social support")

| * [Community-based management](/wiki/Community-based_management "Community-based management") * [Community building](/wiki/Community_building "Community building") * [Community economic development](/wiki/Community_economic_development "Community economic development") * [Community development planning](/wiki/Community_development_planning "Community development planning") * [Community organizing](/wiki/Community_organizing "Community organizing")

is a [social unit](/wiki/Level_of_analysis "Level of analysis") (a group of people) with a shared socially-significant characteristic(s), being [place](/wiki/Place_(geography) "Place (geography)"), set of [norms](/wiki/Norm_(social) "Norm (social)"), [culture](/wiki/Culture "Culture"), [religion](/wiki/Religion "Religion"), [values](/wiki/Values "Values"), [customs](/wiki/Convention_(norm) "Convention (norm)"), or [identity](/wiki/Identity_(social_science) "Identity (social science)"). Communities may share a sense of place situated in a given geographical area (e.g. a [country](/wiki/Country "Country"), [village](/wiki/Village "Village"), [town](/wiki/Town "Town"), or [neighborhood](/wiki/Neighbourhood "Neighbourhood")) or in virtual space through communication platforms. Durable good relations that extend beyond immediate genealogical ties also define a sense of community, important to people's identity, practice, and roles in social [institutions](/wiki/Institution "Institution") such as [family](/wiki/Family "Family"), home, work, [government](/wiki/Government "Government"), [society](/wiki/Society "Society"), or humanity at large.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Although communities are usually small relative to personal social ties, "community" may also refer to large-group affiliations such as [national communities](/wiki/Nation "Nation"), [international communities](/wiki/International_community "International community"), and [virtual communities](/wiki/Virtual_community "Virtual community").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

communities, which are self-sustaining and self-regulating (such as a forest or a lake), and **minor** communities, which rely on other communities (like fungi decomposing a log) and are the building blocks of major communities. Moreover, we can establish other non-taxonomic subdivisions of biocenosis, such as [guilds](/wiki/Guild_(ecology) "Guild (ecology)").

A **community** is a [social unit](/wiki/Level_of_analysis "Level of analysis") (a group of people) with a shared socially-significant characteristic(s), being [place](/wiki/Place_(geography) "Place (geography)"), set of [norms](/wiki/Norm_(social) "Norm (social)"), [culture](/wiki/Culture "Culture"), [religion](/wiki/Religion "Religion"), [values](/wiki/Values "Values"), [customs](/wiki/Convention_(norm) "Convention (norm)"), or [identity](/wiki/Identity_(social_science) "Identity (social science)"). Communities may share a sense of place situated in a given geographical area (e.g. a [country](/wiki/Country "Country"), [village](/wiki/Village "Village"), [town](/wiki/Town "Town"), or [neighborhood](/wiki/Neighbourhood "Neighbourhood")) or in virtual space through communication platforms. Durable good relations that extend beyond immediate genealogical ties also define a sense of community, important to people's identity, practice, and roles in social [institutions](/wiki/Institution "Institution") such as [family](/wiki/Family "Family"), home, work, [government](/wiki/Government "Government"), [society](/wiki/Society "Society"), or humanity at large.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Although communities are usually small relative to personal social ties, "community" may also refer to large-group affiliations such as [national communities](/wiki/Nation "Nation"), [international communities](/wiki/International_community "International community"), and [virtual communities](/wiki/Virtual_community "Virtual community").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

In terms of [sociological](/wiki/Sociological "Sociological") categories, a community can seem like a sub-set of a [social collectivity](/wiki/Social_collectivity "Social collectivity").[[3]](#cite_note-3)
In developmental views, a community can emerge out of a collectivity.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The [English-language](/wiki/English_language "English language") word "community" derives from the [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") *comuneté* ([Modern French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): **communauté**), which comes from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *[communitas](/wiki/Communitas "Communitas")* "community", "public spirit" (from Latin *[communis](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/communis "wikt:communis")*, "common").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

[Human](/wiki/Human "Human") communities may have [intent](/wiki/Intention "Intention"), [belief](/wiki/Belief "Belief"), [resources](/wiki/Natural_resource "Natural resource"), [preferences](/wiki/Preference "Preference"), [needs](/wiki/Need_assessment "Need assessment"), and [risks](/wiki/Risk "Risk") in common, affecting the identity of the participants and their degree of [cohesiveness.](/wiki/Group_cohesiveness "Group cohesiveness")[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Perspectives of various disciplines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Community&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Perspectives of various disciplines")]

### Archaeology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Community&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Archaeology")]

[Archaeological studies](/wiki/Archaeology "Archaeology") of social communities use the term "community" in two ways, mirroring usage in other areas. The first meaning is an informal definition of community as a place where people used to live. In this literal sense it is synonymous with the concept of an ancient [settlement](/wiki/Human_settlement "Human settlement")—whether a [hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet_(place) "Hamlet (place)"), [village](/wiki/Village "Village"), [town](/wiki/Town "Town"), or [city](/wiki/City "City"). The second meaning resembles the usage of the term in other [social sciences](/wiki/Social_sciences "Social sciences"): a community is a group of people living near one another who interact socially. [Social interaction](/wiki/Social_interaction "Social interaction") on a small scale can be difficult to identify with archaeological data. Most reconstructions of social communities by archaeologists rely on the principle that social interaction in the past was conditioned by physical distance. Therefore, a small village settlement likely constituted a social community and spatial subdivisions of cities and other large settlements may have formed communities. [Archaeologists](/wiki/Archaeology "Archaeology") typically use similarities in [material culture](/wiki/Material_culture "Material culture")—from house types to styles of pottery—to reconstruct communities in the past. This classification method relies on the assumption that people or households will share more similarities in the types and styles of their material goods with other members of a social community than they will with outsiders.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Ecology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Community&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ecology")]

Main article: [Community (ecology)](/wiki/Community_(ecology) "Community (ecology)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/IB_Biology_Figure_Project_%281%29.svg/250px-IB_Biology_Figure_Project_%281%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:IB_Biology_Figure_Project_(1).svg)

A simplified example of a community. A community includes many populations and how they interact with each other. This example shows interaction between the zebra and the bush, and between the lion and the zebra, as well as between the bird and the organisms by the water, like the worms.

In [ecology](/wiki/Ecology "Ecology"), a community is an assemblage of populations—potentially of different species—interacting with one another. Community ecology is the branch of ecology that studies interactions between and among species. It considers how such interactions, along with interactions between species and the [abiotic](/wiki/Abiotic "Abiotic") environment, affect social structure and species richness, diversity and patterns of abundance.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Species interact in three ways: [competition](/wiki/Competition "Competition"), [predation](/wiki/Predation "Predation") and [mutualism](/wiki/Mutualism_(biology) "Mutualism (biology)"):

* Competition typically results in a double negative—that is both species lose in the interaction.
* Predation involves a win/lose situation, with one species winning.
* Mutualism sees both species co-operating in some way, with both winning.

The two main types of ecological communities are **major** communities, which are self-sustaining and self-regulating (such as a forest or a lake), and **minor** communities, which rely on other communities (like fungi decomposing a log) and are the building blocks of major communities. Moreover, we can establish other non-taxonomic subdivisions of biocenosis, such as [guilds](/wiki/Guild_(ecology) "Guild (ecology)").

### Philosophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Community&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Philosophy")]

In light of the debate about the meaning of the “[European Community](/wiki/European_Community "European Community"),” a series of European philosophers began to raise concerns with how community has been traditionally conceived in the West. They questioned whether the closed, exclusionary, and identitarian models of community found in the traditions of [Communitarianism](/wiki/Communitarianism "Communitarianism") in Anglo-American philosophy and Classical [Social Theory](/wiki/Social_Theory "Social Theory"), were suitable for our globalized world. However, instead of abandoning the desire to belong in a community, they attempt to reconceptualize community in an open and inclusive manner. [Jean-Luc Nancy](/wiki/Jean-Luc_Nancy "Jean-Luc Nancy") is credited with starting this debate with his book *The Inoperative Community*, followed by [Maurice Blanchot](/wiki/Maurice_Blanchot "Maurice Blanchot")’s *The Unavowable Community*, [Giorgio Agamben](/wiki/Giorgio_Agamben "Giorgio Agamben")’s *The Coming Community*, and [Roberto Esposito](/wiki/Roberto_Esposito "Roberto Esposito")’s *Communitas*.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Jean-Luc Nancy revised his theory of community in *Being Singular Plural*, and he delivered a series of reflections on the terms and motifs of this debate in *The Disavowed Community*.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Other books related to this series include [Zygmunt Bauman](/wiki/Zygmunt_Bauman "Zygmunt Bauman")’s *Community*, Greg Bird’s *Containing Community*, [Miranda Joseph](/wiki/Miranda_Joseph "Miranda Joseph")’s *Against the Romance of Community*, and [Alphonso Lingis](/wiki/Alphonso_Lingis "Alphonso Lingis")’ *The Community of Those Who Have Nothing in Common*.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Semantics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Community&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Semantics")]

The concept of "community" often has a positive semantic connotation, exploited rhetorically by populist politicians and by advertisers[[12]](#cite_note-12)
to promote feelings and associations of mutual well-being, happiness and togetherness[[13]](#cite_note-13)—veering towards an almost-achievable [utopian community](/wiki/Utopian_community "Utopian community").

In contrast, the [epidemiological](/wiki/Epidemiology "Epidemiology") term "[community transmission](/wiki/Community_transmission "Community transmission")" can have negative implications,[[14]](#cite_note-14) and instead of a "criminal community"[[15]](#cite_note-15) one often speaks of a "[criminal underworld](/wiki/Criminal_underworld "Criminal underworld")" or of the "criminal fraternity".

### Sociology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Community&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Sociology")]

Early sociological studies identified communities as fringe groups at the behest of local power elites. Such early academic studies include *[Who Governs?](/wiki/Who_Governs%3F "Who Governs?")* by [Robert Dahl](/wiki/Robert_Dahl "Robert Dahl") as well as the papers by [Floyd Hunter](/w/index.php?title=Floyd_Hunter&action=edit&redlink=1 "Floyd Hunter (page does not exist)") on [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"). At the turn of the 21st century the concept of community was rediscovered by academics, politicians, and activists. Politicians hoping for a democratic election started to realign with community interests.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### Others

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Community&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Others")]

The [Shona](/wiki/Shona_people "Shona people") (in Zimbabwe) include [ancestral spirits](/wiki/Ancestral_spirits "Ancestral spirits") (*midzimu*) in their conceptualisation of the community.[[17]](#cite_note-:1-17)

## Key concepts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Community&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Key concepts")]

### *Gemeinschaft and Gesellschaft*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Community&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Gemeinschaft and Gesellschaft")]

Main article: [Gemeinschaft and Gesellschaft](/wiki/Gemeinschaft_and_Gesellschaft "Gemeinschaft and Gesellschaft")

In *Gemeinschaft und Gesellschaft* (1887), German sociologist [Ferdinand Tönnies](/wiki/Ferdinand_T%C3%B6nnies "Ferdinand Tönnies") described two types of human association: *Gemeinschaft* (usually translated as "community") and *Gesellschaft* ("society" or "association"). Tönnies proposed the **Gemeinschaft*–**Gesellschaft* [dichotomy](/wiki/Dichotomy "Dichotomy") as a way to think about social ties. No group is exclusively one or the other. *Gemeinschaft* stress personal [social interactions](/wiki/Social_interaction "Social interaction"), and the roles, values, and beliefs based on such interactions. *Gesellschaft* stress indirect interactions, impersonal roles, formal values, and beliefs based on such interactions.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Sense of community

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Community&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Sense of community")]

Main article: [Sense of community](/wiki/Sense_of_community "Sense of community")

In a seminal 1986 study, McMillan and Chavis[[19]](#cite_note-19) identify four elements of "sense of community":

1. membership: feeling of belonging or of sharing a sense of personal relatedness,
2. influence: mattering, making a difference to a group and of the group mattering to its members
3. reinforcement: integration and fulfillment of needs,
4. shared emotional connection.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Bigdayout_crowd2.jpg/250px-Bigdayout_crowd2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bigdayout_crowd2.jpg)

To what extent do participants in joint activities experience a [sense of community](/wiki/Sense_of_community "Sense of community")?

A "sense of community index" (SCI) was developed by Chavis and colleagues, and revised and adapted by others. Although originally designed to assess sense of community in neighborhoods, the index has been adapted for use in schools, the workplace, and a variety of types of communities.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Studies conducted by the American Psychological Association indicate that young adults who feel a sense of belonging in a community, particularly small communities, develop fewer psychiatric and depressive disorders than those who do not have the feeling of love and belonging.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

#### Socialization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Community&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Socialization")]

Main article: [Socialization](/wiki/Socialization "Socialization")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Lewes_Bonfire%2C_Martyrs_Crosses.jpg/250px-Lewes_Bonfire%2C_Martyrs_Crosses.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lewes_Bonfire,_Martyrs_Crosses.jpg)

[Lewes Bonfire Night](/wiki/Lewes#Lewes_Bonfire "Lewes") procession commemorating 17 Protestant martyrs burnt at the stake from 1555 to 1557

The process of learning to adopt the [behavior](/wiki/Behavior "Behavior") patterns of the community is called [socialization](/wiki/Socialization "Socialization"). The most fertile time of socialization is usually the early stages of life, during which [individuals](/wiki/Individual "Individual") develop the skills and knowledge and learn the [roles](/wiki/Role "Role") necessary to function within their [culture](/wiki/Culture "Culture") and [social environment](/wiki/Social_environment "Social environment").[[22]](#cite_note-Socialization-22) For some psychologists, especially those in the [psychodynamic](/wiki/Psychodynamic "Psychodynamic") tradition, the most important period of socialization is between the ages of one and ten. But socialization also includes adults moving into a significantly different environment where they must learn a new set of behaviors.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Socialization is influenced primarily by the family, through which children first learn community [norms](/wiki/Norm_(sociology) "Norm (sociology)"). Other important influences include schools, [peer](/wiki/Peer_group "Peer group") groups, people, mass media, the [workplace](/wiki/Workplace "Workplace"), and government. The degree to which the norms of a particular society or community are adopted determines one's willingness to engage with others. The norms of [tolerance](/wiki/Toleration "Toleration"), [reciprocity](/wiki/Reciprocity_(social_psychology) "Reciprocity (social psychology)"), and [trust](/wiki/Trust_(sociology) "Trust (sociology)") are important "habits of the heart", as [de Tocqueville](/wiki/Alexis_de_Tocqueville "Alexis de Tocqueville") put it, in an individual's involvement in community.[[24]](#cite_note-Community-24)

## Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Community&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Development")]

Main article: [Community development](/wiki/Community_development "Community development")

Community development is often linked with [community work](/wiki/Community_practice "Community practice") or community planning, and may involve stakeholders, foundations, governments, or contracted entities including [non-government organisations](/wiki/Non-government_organisations "Non-government organisations") (NGOs), universities or government agencies to progress the social well-being of local, regional and, sometimes, national communities. More grassroots efforts, called [community building](/wiki/Community_building "Community building") or [community organizing](/wiki/Community_organizing "Community organizing"), seek to empower individuals and groups of people by providing them with the skills they need to effect change in their own communities.[[25]](#cite_note-25) These skills often assist in building political power through the formation of large social groups working for a common agenda. Community development practitioners understand how to work with individuals and affect communities' positions within the context of larger social institutions. Public administrators, in contrast, understand community development in the context of rural and urban development, housing and economic development, and community, organizational and business development.

Formal accredited programs conducted by universities, as part of degree granting institutions, are often used to build a knowledge base to drive curricula in [public administration](/wiki/Public_administration "Public administration"), [sociology](/wiki/Sociology "Sociology") and [community studies](/wiki/Community_studies "Community studies"). The [General Social Survey](/wiki/General_Social_Survey "General Social Survey") from the [National Opinion Research Center](/wiki/National_Opinion_Research_Center "National Opinion Research Center") at the [University of Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago") and the [Saguaro Seminar](/wiki/Saguaro_Seminar "Saguaro Seminar") at the [Harvard Kennedy School](/wiki/Harvard_Kennedy_School "Harvard Kennedy School") are examples of national community development in the United States. The [Maxwell School of Citizenship and Public Affairs](/wiki/Maxwell_School_of_Citizenship_and_Public_Affairs "Maxwell School of Citizenship and Public Affairs") at Syracuse University in New York State offers core courses in community and economic development, and in areas ranging from non-profit development to US budgeting (federal to local, community funds). In the United Kingdom, the [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") has led in providing extensive research in the field through its *Community Development Journal,*[[26]](#cite_note-26) used worldwide by sociologists and community development practitioners.

At the intersection between community *development* and community *building* are a number of programs and organizations with community development tools. One example of this is the program of the [Asset Based Community Development](/wiki/Asset-based_community_development "Asset-based community development") Institute of [Northwestern University](/wiki/Northwestern_University "Northwestern University"). The institute makes available downloadable tools[[27]](#cite_note-27) to assess community assets and make connections between [non-profit groups](/wiki/Non-profit_organization "Non-profit organization") and other organizations that can help in community building. The Institute focuses on helping communities develop by "mobilizing neighborhood assets" – building from the inside out rather than the outside in.[[28]](#cite_note-28) In the disability field, community building was prevalent in the 1980s and 1990s with roots in John McKnight's approaches.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Building and organizing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Community&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Building and organizing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Affinity_group_collateral_damage.jpg/250px-Affinity_group_collateral_damage.jpg)](/wiki/File:Affinity_group_collateral_damage.jpg)

The anti-war [affinity group](/wiki/Affinity_group "Affinity group") "Collateral Damage" [protesting](/wiki/Activism "Activism") the [Iraq War](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War")

In *The Different Drum: Community-Making and Peace* (1987) [Scott Peck](/wiki/M._Scott_Peck "M. Scott Peck") argues that the almost accidental sense of community that exists at times of crisis can be consciously built. Peck believes that conscious community building is a process of deliberate design based on the knowledge and application of certain rules.[[31]](#cite_note-31) He states that this process goes through four stages:[[32]](#cite_note-32)

1. **Pseudocommunity**: When people first come together, they try to be "nice" and present what they feel are their most personable and friendly characteristics.
2. **Chaos**: People move beyond the inauthenticity of pseudo-community and feel safe enough to present their "shadow" selves.
3. **Emptiness**: Moves beyond the attempts to fix, heal and convert of the chaos stage, when all people become capable of acknowledging their own woundedness and brokenness, common to human beings.
4. **True community**: Deep respect and true listening for the needs of the other people in this community.

In 1991, Peck remarked that building a sense of community is easy but maintaining this sense of community is difficult in the modern world.[[33]](#cite_note-33) An interview with M. Scott Peck by Alan Atkisson. *In Context* #29, p. 26.
The three basic types of community organizing are [grassroots](/wiki/Grassroots "Grassroots") organizing, [coalition](/wiki/Coalition "Coalition") building, and "institution-based community organizing", (also called "broad-based community organizing", an example of which is [faith-based community organizing](/wiki/Faith-based_community "Faith-based community"), or [Congregation-based Community Organizing](/wiki/Congregation-based_Community_Organizing "Congregation-based Community Organizing")).[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Community building can use a wide variety of practices, ranging from simple events (e.g., [potlucks](/wiki/Potluck "Potluck"), small [book clubs](/wiki/Book_discussion_club "Book discussion club")) to larger-scale efforts (e.g., mass [festivals](/wiki/Festival "Festival"), [construction](/wiki/Construction "Construction") projects that involve local participants rather than outside contractors).

Community building that is geared toward citizen action is usually termed "community organizing".[[35]](#cite_note-organizing-35) In these cases, organized community groups seek accountability from elected officials and increased direct representation within decision-making bodies. Where good-faith negotiations fail, these constituency-led organizations seek to pressure the decision-makers through a variety of means, including picketing, [boycotting](/wiki/Boycott "Boycott"), sit-ins, petitioning, and electoral politics.

Community organizing can focus on more than just resolving specific issues. Organizing often means building a widely accessible power structure, often with the end goal of distributing power equally throughout the community. Community organizers generally seek to build groups that are open and democratic in governance. Such groups facilitate and encourage [consensus decision-making](/wiki/Consensus_decision-making "Consensus decision-making") with a focus on the general health of the community rather than a specific interest group.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

If communities are developed based on something they share in common, whether location or values, then one challenge for developing communities is how to incorporate individuality and differences. Rebekah Nathan suggests in her book, *My Freshman Year*, we are drawn to developing communities totally based on sameness, despite stated commitments to diversity, such as those found on university websites.

## Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Community&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Types")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Stonehenge_Summer_Solstice_eve_02.jpg/250px-Stonehenge_Summer_Solstice_eve_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stonehenge_Summer_Solstice_eve_02.jpg)

A [community of interest](/wiki/Community_of_interest "Community of interest") gathers at [Stonehenge](/wiki/Stonehenge "Stonehenge"), England, for the summer solstice.

A number of ways to categorize types of community have been proposed. One such breakdown is as follows:

1. **Location-based**: range from the local [neighbourhood](/wiki/Neighbourhood "Neighbourhood"), [suburb](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb"), [village](/wiki/Village "Village"), [town](/wiki/Town "Town") or [city](/wiki/City "City"), region, nation or even the planet as a whole. These are also called **communities of place**.
2. **Identity-based**: range from the local clique, sub-culture, [ethnic group](/wiki/Ethnic_group "Ethnic group"), [religious](/wiki/Religious "Religious"), [multicultural](/wiki/Multiculturalism "Multiculturalism") or [pluralistic](/wiki/Pluralism_(political_philosophy) "Pluralism (political philosophy)") [civilisation](/wiki/Civilisation "Civilisation"), or the [global](/wiki/Globalisation "Globalisation") community cultures of today. They may be included as *communities of need* or *identity*, such as [disabled persons](/wiki/Disability "Disability"), or [frail aged](/wiki/Senior_Citizens "Senior Citizens") people.
3. **Organizationally-based**: range from communities organized informally around [family](/wiki/Family "Family") or [network](/wiki/Social_network "Social network")-based guilds and associations to more formal [incorporated associations](/wiki/Incorporation_(business) "Incorporation (business)"), [political](/wiki/Politics "Politics") [decision-making](/wiki/Decision-making "Decision-making") structures, [economic](/wiki/Economics "Economics") enterprises, or professional associations at a small, national or international scale.
4. **[Intentional](/wiki/Intentional_community "Intentional community")**: a mix of all three previous types, these are highly cohesive residential communities with a common social or spiritual purpose, ranging from [monasteries](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery") and [ashrams](/wiki/Ashram "Ashram") to modern [ecovillages](/wiki/Ecovillage "Ecovillage") and [housing cooperatives](/wiki/Housing_cooperative "Housing cooperative").

The usual categorizations of community relations have a number of problems:[[37]](#cite_note-37) (1) they tend to give the impression that a particular community can be defined as just this kind or another; (2) they tend to conflate modern and customary community relations; (3) they tend to take sociological categories such as ethnicity or race as given, forgetting that different ethnically defined persons live in different kinds of communities—grounded, interest-based, diasporic, etc.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

In response to these problems, [Paul James](/wiki/Paul_James_(academic) "Paul James (academic)") and his colleagues have developed a [taxonomy](/wiki/Taxonomy "Taxonomy") that maps community relations, and recognizes that actual communities can be characterized by different kinds of relations at the same time:[[39]](#cite_note-39)

1. **Grounded community relations**. This involves enduring attachment to particular places and particular people. It is the dominant form taken by customary and [tribal communities](/wiki/Tribal_communities "Tribal communities"). In these kinds of communities, the land is fundamental to identity.
2. **Life-style community relations**. This involves giving primacy to communities coming together around particular chosen ways of life, such as morally charged or interest-based relations or just living or working in the same location. Hence the following sub-forms:
   1. community-life as morally bounded, a form taken by many traditional faith-based communities.
   2. community-life as interest-based, including sporting, leisure-based and business communities which come together for regular moments of engagement.
   3. community-life as proximately-related, where neighbourhood or commonality of association forms a community of convenience, or a [community of place](/wiki/Community_of_place "Community of place") (see below).
3. **Projected community relations**. This is where a community is self-consciously treated as an entity to be projected and re-created. It can be projected as through thin advertising slogan, for example [gated community](/wiki/Gated_community "Gated community"), or can take the form of ongoing associations of people who seek political integration, [communities of practice](/wiki/Communities_of_practice "Communities of practice")[[40]](#cite_note-40) based on professional projects, associative communities which seek to enhance and support individual creativity, autonomy and mutuality. A [nation](/wiki/Nation "Nation") is one of the largest forms of projected or [imagined community](/wiki/Imagined_community "Imagined community").

In these terms, communities can be nested and/or intersecting; one community can contain another—for example a location-based community may contain a number of [ethnic communities](/wiki/Ethnic_communities "Ethnic communities").[[41]](#cite_note-41) Both lists above can be used in a cross-cutting matrix in relation to each other.

## Internet communities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Community&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Internet communities")]

Main article: [Virtual community](/wiki/Virtual_community "Virtual community")

In general, [virtual communities](/wiki/Virtual_community "Virtual community") value knowledge and information as [currency](/wiki/Social_currency "Social currency") or social resource.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) What differentiates virtual communities from their physical counterparts is the extent and impact of "weak ties", which are the relationships acquaintances or strangers form to acquire information through online networks.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Relationships among members in a [virtual community](/wiki/Virtual_community "Virtual community") tend to focus on information exchange about specific topics.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) A survey conducted by [Pew Internet](/wiki/Pew_Research_Centre "Pew Research Centre") and The American Life Project in 2001 found those involved in entertainment, professional, and sports virtual-groups focused their activities on obtaining information.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

An epidemic of [bullying](/wiki/Cyberbullying "Cyberbullying") and harassment has arisen from the exchange of information between strangers, especially among teenagers,[[50]](#cite_note-50) in virtual communities. Despite attempts to implement anti-bullying policies, Sheri Bauman, professor of counselling at the University of Arizona, claims the "most effective strategies to prevent bullying" may cost companies revenue.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

Virtual Internet-mediated communities can interact with offline [real-life](/wiki/Real-life "Real-life") activity, potentially forming strong and tight-knit groups such as [QAnon](/wiki/QAnon "QAnon").[[52]](#cite_note-52)

People who spend most of their time in internet communities are generally known to be more [Homosexual](/wiki/Homosexuality "Homosexuality") on average over the general population of people who socialize in offline, [real life](/wiki/Real_life "Real life") situations.[[53]](#cite_note-53)
